schools.summary: Ellicott: Jehovah, not Rimmon, gives Syria her victories; with the Midrash he and Clarke make Naaman the archer who slew Ahab.
Henry: no greatness sets a man beyond calamity's reach; every man has 'some but or other,' and the leprosy hides pride from him.
Gill fixes the setting under Benhadad, though some read Hazael as the reigning Syrian.
Clarke: God pairs great defects with great eminence to keep proud men sober.
K&D and Ellicott read 'gedudim' as marauding bands, not an army, and set the capture amid the slave-traffic of the age.
Clarke marvels at 'the goodness and severity' of providence: the child robbed of parents is not left without a guardian.
Gill: the Syrians were not regular troops but a banditti of robbers plundering Israel's border.
K&D and Ellicott parse 'asaph' ('recover from leprosy') as receiving the leper back into fellowship, as with Miriam.
Clarke: by the little maid's captivity 'the mystery of Divine providence begins to develop itself' — a Syrian house is brought to the true God.
Gill pictures the maid speaking hope into her mistress's despair over an incurable case.

debates.question: What does the verb 'recover from leprosy' properly mean?

sides.position: to receive the leper back into fellowship (hence, to heal)
to take a person away from the leprosy to which he was delivered up

schools.summary: Gill: with most Jewish commentators, it was Naaman himself (told by his wife) who reported the maid's words to the king.
Ellicott: 'he went in' is Naaman, though some MSS read 'she went in'; 'thus and thus' avoids repeating her words.
Clarke marvels that the maid conducted herself so well that a royal embassy was founded on her word.

debates.question: Who carried the maid's report to the king of Syria?

sides.position: Naaman himself, informed by his wife
a servant, or (per some MSS) the mistress

schools.summary: K&D take the huge present as fitting Naaman's rank; Ellicott judges the numbers 'probably corrupt' and too large; both weigh the sums and the raiment.
Wesley: the king naturally assumed Israel's king could do more than a subject; Clarke reckons the gift a princely token of Naaman's esteem.
Gill: the king urged Naaman to go directly to Samaria.

debates.question: Are the sums recorded historically reliable?

sides.position: fitting Naaman's rank and the object in view
the total is too large; the numbers are probably corrupt

schools.summary: Ellicott: the friendly tone dates the events to Jehoram's reign, in the lull after Ramoth-gilead; against Schenkel, who assigns them to Jehu's reign with Hazael as the writer.
Gill: only the part of the letter touching Naaman's case is recorded.

debates.question: Under which reign do these events fall?

sides.position: the reign of Jehoram, in the lull after Ramoth-gilead
the reign of Jehu, with Hazael as the letter's author

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott: 'Am I God to kill and make alive?' echoes Deuteronomy and Samuel; leprosy is a living death, and the king rends his clothes as at blasphemy.
Clarke: all nations held that God alone cures leprosy, so the king reads the demand as a pretext for war.
Gill: the king rends his clothes in distress, whether at the blasphemy or in perplexity.

themes: God alone kills and makes alive

schools.summary: Ellicott stresses 'there is a prophet' and asks why Jehoram forgot Elisha: strained relations, no precedent, and sheer fright.
Clarke hears Elisha's message as reassurance: the matter belongs to the prophet of the Most High.
Gill: Elisha acts on the report of the king's public despair.

themes: there is a prophet in Israel

schools.summary: Henry: Naaman waits at the door 'as a beggar,' as all must wait at Wisdom's gate; Elisha keeps aloof to humble his pride and refuse flattery.
K&D reject the legal, fearful, and miracle-magnifying explanations, tracing Elisha's aloofness to Naaman's pride; Ellicott denies any pride and appeals to reverence and convenience.
Gill allows several motives: retirement for prayer and a holy contempt of worldly grandeur.

debates.question: Why did Elisha stay indoors and send a messenger instead of coming out?

sides.position: to humble Naaman's pride and keep the cure free of flattery
not pride at all — reverence held petitioners back, and a messenger was at hand
the ceremonial ban on touching lepers

schools.summary: Ellicott and K&D: the Jordan has no healing virtue; the bare command shows the cure is God's, at the prophet's prayer, the flesh 'returning' clean.
Wesley: the command exercises Naaman's faith and honours true religion; Clarke: God may convey healing by whatever means he pleases.
Gill: Elisha stays retired, perhaps at prayer, and to mortify Naaman's pride.
Clarke unfolds Naaman's four-clause fancy of how the cure should be done; Wesley: he was wroth, supposing himself despised.
Ellicott: the expected gesture was to 'wave the hand toward the place,' Naaman assuming even the healer would not touch the unclean spot.
Gill: Naaman was angry on more accounts than one.
Ellicott identifies Abana/Amana as the Barada and Pharpar as the Nahr el-Awaj, clear mountain streams beside the turbid Jordan; Clarke and Gill cite Maundrell.
Wesley: the rivers may have equal virtue, but the cure was to be wrought not by the water but by the power of God.
Henry: Jordan belonged to Israel's God; relation to God matters more than the beauty of the stream, and proud men forsake their own mercies.

themes: the water or the power of God

schools.summary: Clarke unfolds the servants' a-fortiori argument: if a hard task, how much more an easy one; Wesley reads 'our father' as reverence and affection.
Henry: a great mercy to have inferiors who will freely reprove us; the plain remedy — believe, repent, wash — leaves refusal inexcusable.
Ellicott: 'my father' implies respect and affection, though the word might be read 'if.'
K&D and Ellicott: sevenfold dipping stamps the cure as God's work and covenant sign; Ellicott cites an Assyrian parallel to bathing for a cure.
Clarke marvels at flesh restored to youth — how mighty is God, by the feeblest of means.
Henry: Naaman dipped 'as lightly as he could,' with little faith, yet God honoured his word; the sinner must not alter the Saviour's directions.

themes: faith alone in the appointed way

schools.summary: Henry: a noble confession, like the returning Samaritan leper; the mercy of the cure moved him more than the miracle.
Ellicott: Naaman's 'no God but in Israel' still betrays a local conception of deity — a mark of historic truth; K&D: Elisha refuses the gift to avoid the false prophets' selfishness.
Clarke: the once-proud captain now stands truly humbled, having left his state behind.

debates.question: How complete is Naaman's new confession of God?

sides.position: a noble, genuine confession born of experienced grace
genuine yet still marked by a local, geographically bounded idea of deity

schools.summary: Wesley and Clarke: not that gifts are unlawful (Elisha took them elsewhere), but to keep the glory God's and let the Syrians see his ministers' holy contempt of wealth.
Ellicott (with Theodoret) likens it to 'freely ye received, freely give'; the refusal, unlike ordinary prophets, deeply impressed Naaman.
Gill: Elisha swears by the Lord whose prophet he is; K&D note his firmness under all Naaman's urging.
Ellicott and K&D: Naaman, with his local theology, wants Israelite soil to build an altar to Jehovah on Jehovah's own land.
Clarke and Wesley: 'earth' means the silver and gold, now despised as dust, offered to the prophet's servant — no altar could be built of two mules' load.
Henry: Naaman 'over-does it' in over-valuing the soil, forgetting all the earth is the Lord's, yet the vow to sacrifice to none but Jehovah is genuine.

debates.question: What is the 'two mules' burden of earth' Naaman asks for?

sides.position: literal Israelite soil, to build or found an altar to Jehovah
the silver and gold, called 'earth' in scorn, given to the prophet's servant

schools.summary: Ellicott: Rimmon is the storm-god Rammanu/Hadad, and 'leaning on the hand' the attendance of a grandee; the request is not to be judged by a Christian standard.
Clarke, with Lightfoot, reads the verbs as past — pardon for former idolatry, not future license, lest it grant an 'indulgence'; Wesley notes the king leaning for state or support.
Henry: the reservation is not justifiable; a reserved known sin is a defeasance of God's covenant — except no house of Rimmon.

debates.question: Is Naaman asking pardon for past idolatry or leave for future compromise?

sides.position: for past acts — the verbs are past tense, not a plea for future license
for a future, unavoidable appearance — Elisha neither approves nor condemns

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'a little way' is a 'kibrah' of ground, a vague distance — not a parasang, or Gehazi could not have overtaken him.
Clarke reports the De Rossi variant reading 'no' for 'to him,' turning Elisha's answer into a refusal of the Rimmon compromise.
Gill simply takes the prophet's word as 'Go in peace' to Naaman.

debates.question: Does 'Go in peace' allow the compromise or forbid it?

sides.position: a forbearing blessing that leaves the matter to God
per the De Rossi variant, a firm 'No' against worship in Rimmon

schools.summary: Henry: the love of money is the root of Gehazi's sin; the means of grace cannot give grace, and he risks alienating the new convert from the faith.
K&D and Ellicott render Gehazi's oath ('but I will run') and hear him justify greed as 'spoiling the Egyptians,' with scorn for 'this Syrian.'
Wesley: to swear to a wicked act does not excuse but aggravates it.

themes: the means of grace cannot give grace

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott: Naaman springs from his chariot in reverence, 'is all well?' — an Oriental mark of respect and anxiety for the prophet.
Clarke: Naaman treats even the servant with the profoundest respect, alighting to meet him.
Gill: Gehazi followed after Naaman as fast as he could.
Ellicott and Clarke: 'mount Ephraim' is the hill-country where a school of the prophets likely stood; the 'changes of garments' echo the embassy's present.
Gill notes the servile smoothness with which Gehazi sets Naaman at ease before his lie.
Ellicott and Clarke: Naaman doubles the gift and has his own servants carry two heavy talents before Gehazi — a courtesy turned to plunder.
Wesley: Gehazi first refused 'upon a pretence of modesty' before taking all.
Gill glosses 'be content' as 'be pleased; do not object.'
K&D and Ellicott read the 'ophel' as the hill/mound by Samaria, a fortified height; Ellicott makes the note of place a mark of historical truth.
Clarke: 'ophel' means a dark hiding place — a deed of darkness seeking darkness, yet found out by the prophet's discerning spirit.
Gill: the tower was a guarded, fortified place near Samaria, safe for the hidden goods.
K&D and Ellicott: Gehazi denies all ('went not hither nor thither'), and Elisha's 'went not my heart with thee?' means the prophet was present in spirit, per Theodoret and the Vulgate.
Gill: Gehazi stands before his master 'as if he had never been from the house.'
K&D and Ellicott: 'went not my heart with thee?' is severe irony — the prophet present in spirit; Ellicott: this was no time to seek gain from a heathen convert.
Clarke and Wesley: Elisha names Gehazi's very plans (oliveyards, vineyards, servants), knowing his most secret intentions; folly to presume on secrecy.
Gill gathers the Targum's 'by a spirit of prophecy it was shown unto me.'

themes: is it a time to receive?
conscience and the eye of God

schools.summary: Clarke catalogues Gehazi's sevenfold guilt and reads 'for ever' (leolam) as lasting till his line dies out; Wesley: those who gain money displeasing to God make a dear purchase.
Ellicott: leprosy is hereditary, and Scripture never spares covetousness and lying; K&D: the punishment struck the abuse of the prophet's name, not the greed alone.
Henry: what was Gehazi profited, gaining two talents but losing health, honour, peace, and perhaps his soul? Treasure got by a lying tongue is a fleeting vanity.
